🚀 When Workflow Automation meets AI, the real unlock for businesses
For years, my work revolved around designing business workflows and repetitive tasks into reliable, predictable systems.
But something has fundamentally changed.
With AI in the loop, automation is no longer just "executing steps." Systems can now analyze, decide, and recommend, while humans stay in control of what matters most.
I recently built an AI-Native Workflow Automation Platform to prove exactly this idea 👇
The use case: a real business scenario, subscription & contract management (energy contracts in this demo, but the pattern applies to insurance, telecom, SaaS, procurement, and more).
The flow:
Input Validation → Data Normalization → Automated Competitor Scraping → AI Analysis → Decision → Human-in-the-Loop Approval → Execution
Under the hood:
🔹 .NET 8 + Clean Architecture + CQRS (maintainable, testable, production-ready)
🔹 Angular 17 frontend with a clean operator UI
🔹 Microsoft Semantic Kernel + OpenAI for the AI reasoning layer
🔹 Windmill.dev for distributed workflow execution (with graceful fallback to in-process .NET)
🔹 Playwright for browser automation & data scraping
🔹 PostgreSQL + structured logging with Serilog
🔹 Deterministic fallback logic when AI is unavailable, so the business never stops
Why this matters 👇
Most companies, from 10-person startups to 10,000-person enterprises, are still drowning in repetitive work:
⚙️ Invoice & document processing
⚙️ Customer onboarding
⚙️ Contract analysis & renewals
⚙️ Vendor comparison & procurement
⚙️ Internal reporting
⚙️ Email triage & classification
The combination of Workflow Orchestration + AI + Human-in-the-Loop can automate 70–90% of these tasks, without removing human judgment from critical decisions.
💼 I'm now open to new opportunities (remote, worldwide).
If you're a founder, hiring manager, operations lead, or CTO, I'd love to hear what you're working on and how I can help.
📩 DM me on LinkedIn, drop a comment below, or find my email and source code in the first comment. 👇

9 things every CFO should be doing in Claude this month:

1. Run a 30-minute health check on Q1 actuals before your team builds the reforecast.

2. Stress-test one assumption you're worried about. Don't update the model yet. Just see the analysis.

3. Build a board deck draft from your last quarter's actuals. Six slides max. Edit from there.

4. Upload your last 3 board decks and ask Claude what story your board has been telling itself about the business. The pattern will surprise you.

5. Drop your customer concentration data in and ask: "What's the worst case if we lose our top 3 customers in the next 12 months?"

6. Upload your vendor contracts and ask Claude to flag any auto-renewals in the next 90 days.

7. Build a one-page hiring brief for your next finance hire that includes their AI workflow as a required interview question.

8. Run a sentiment analysis on your last earnings call transcript. Find the exact moments your tone changed.

9. Ask Claude to write your CEO a memo on what you'd do differently in the next 6 months if you had 50% of the budget you have today.

Nine workflows. Two hours. Each one returns your time investment within a week.

Save this post. Run them this month.

The $5M Lesson in "Local-Only" Hiring

Last year, a colleague’s fintech firm had a perfect plan for an AI risk tool. The budget was there, but they insisted on a "local-only" hiring policy.
Six months later, the project was still a slide deck.

While they spent half a year cycling through local recruiters and "ghosted" interviews, a smaller competitor in Europe took a different path. They ignored geography and tapped into a global network of specialized MLOps and RAG architects immediately.

By the time my colleague made his first local hire, the competitor had already shipped V2 and snatched a $5M contract from right under his nose.
The 2026 "Wait-and-See" Tax

In a market where AI demand exceeds supply by 3.2 to 1, waiting 180 days to fill a seat isn't just a delay—it’s an existential risk. This is the "Wait-and-See" tax: measured in lost market share and burnt-out founders.

The leaders winning in 2026 have shifted their mindset:

The desk location doesn't matter; the expertise does.
Hyper-specialization beats generalism. You don't need a "dev"; you need an LLM fine-tuning expert.

Speed is the only moat. Getting a vetted expert in the seat in 48 hours is worth more than a local hire in 4 months.

The global talent gap is a chasm, but you don’t have to fall into it. Your customers don’t care where your code was written—they only care that it works and that you got there first.

We’ve reached a bizarre era in hiring. You beat the AI, you pass the filters, and your reward? An invitation to "Breadcrumb Island."

Leaders: Are you vetting talent, or just generating static?

Guys, I did it. 
My green banner is gone! 🥹 More to follow on that later, but for anyone looking for work, or interested in the data relating to the current job market climate here are the numbers of my experience.

🕰️ It has taken me 9 weeks of applying, interviewing & active outreach at full time hours to secure an offer.

💻 422 Job applications. Using a combination of fully tailored CV's & cover letters both with & without AI (love a bit of A/B testing). Here is what it resulted in: 

⛔ 147 Rejection emails. (ouch)
👻 263 Applications ghosted entirely. (ewwww)
👋 9 Recruiter's engaged (and many more calls & emails with each).
👩‍💻 Interviews - 12 First stage interviews. 5 Second stage. 2 Third stage. 1 Fourth stage. 1 Fifth stage.- 4 in person interviews, 5 AI interviews, 4 'Personality' tests & a one hour presentation.
🧾 Two job offers, landed last week, decision made & contract signed this weekend. 

🦵 The kicker was NEITHER job offer came from an application or recruiter. One was a reply to a post I sent to a founder, the other was the CEO of the organisation reaching out to me directly after seeing a post I wrote - both via LinkedIn. 

🫶 I guess my lesson learned here is the hiring process is changing fast, personal connection is more important than ever in this sea of AI job posts, CV's, Applications & Interviews. Putting time into posts, comments and active DM outreach is ultimately what closed this job search for me in the end. (thank goodness). 

Very excited to share what's next for me soon! 👀

𝘚𝘵𝘰𝘱 𝘏𝘪𝘳𝘪𝘯𝘨 ‘𝘗𝘳𝘰𝘮𝘱𝘵 𝘌𝘯𝘨𝘪𝘯𝘦𝘦𝘳𝘴’ - 𝘠𝘰𝘶 𝘕𝘦𝘦𝘥 𝘊𝘩𝘦𝘧𝘴 🧑‍🍳 

There's an identity crisis happening in tech right now.

Someone just posted a job for a "Prompt Engineer" that wanted:
Distributed systems + AI design + ML + Ops + Security + Product.

That's not a role. That's five people stuffed into a job title from 2023.

Here’s the truth no one’s saying out loud:
Prompt engineering was never the endgame. It was the recipe.

𝗔𝗴𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗲𝗻𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗶𝘀 𝗯𝗲𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗰𝗵𝗲𝗳.

And if you're hiring or building > for real-world agents that actually survive production, you need these 7 skills. 

Not vibes. 
Not better sentences.

🧠 𝟭. 𝗦𝘆𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗺 𝗱𝗲𝘀𝗶𝗴𝗻
You're not building a chatbot. You're building an orchestra. LLMs, tools, memory, sub-agents. If you can't trace data flow and failure modes, you're done.

🔧 𝟮. 𝗧𝗼𝗼𝗹 & 𝗰𝗼𝗻𝘁𝗿𝗮𝗰𝘁 𝗱𝗲𝘀𝗶𝗴𝗻
Vague schema = agent imagination. And imagination + financial transactions = disaster. Tight contracts or go home.

📚 𝟯. 𝗥𝗲𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗲𝘃𝗮𝗹 𝗲𝗻𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴
RAG sounds simple. It’s not. Chunk size, embeddings, reranking garbage in, confident garbage out.

🛡️ 𝟰. 𝗥𝗲𝗹𝗶𝗮𝗯𝗶𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆 𝗲𝗻𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴
APIs fail. Services timeout. Retries, backoffs, circuit breakers. Backend 101. Most agent builders skipped that class. Don't be them.

🔐 𝟱. 𝗦𝗲𝗰𝘂𝗿𝗶𝘁𝘆 & 𝘀𝗮𝗳𝗲𝘁𝘆
Prompt injection is real. "Ignore previous instructions" still works on way too many agents. Input validation, output filters, least privilege. Now.

📊 𝟲. 𝗘𝘃𝗮𝗹𝘂𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 & 𝗼𝗯𝘀𝗲𝗿𝘃𝗮𝗯𝗶𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆
"You cannot improve what you cannot measure." Tracing, logging, test suites. Vibes don't deploy. Metrics do.

👥 𝟳. 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗱𝘂𝗰𝘁 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗸𝗶𝗻𝗴
When does your agent say "I don't know"? When does it escalate to a human? Build trust, not error messages. Humans are part of the system.

𝗧𝗼 𝗲𝗻𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗿𝘀:
Stop tweaking prompts. Trace one failure back. Was it retrieval? Tool schema? Missing timeout? Fix the system, not the sentence.

𝗧𝗼 𝗵𝗶𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗺𝗮𝗻𝗮𝗴𝗲𝗿𝘀:
If your job description still says "Prompt Engineer" and expects a unicorn, rewrite it. Hire for system thinking + tool design + reliability. Crank Recruitment can help you find the people who actually build agents that work.

𝗪𝗲 𝗱𝗼𝗻'𝘁 𝗷𝘂𝘀𝘁 𝗿𝗲𝗰𝗿𝘂𝗶𝘁 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝘁𝗲𝗰𝗵. 𝗪𝗲 𝘂𝗻𝗱𝗲𝗿𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗶𝘁.

And the gap between “works in demo” and “survives production” is where great engineers separate from the noise.

Q1 2026 numbers are in and they're telling. Here's what's been moving in the HealthTech world this week 👇

💰 Funding - Rock Health dropped its Q1 report this week and the headline is hard to ignore. Digital health startups raised $4B across 110 deals in Q1 2026, up $1B on the same quarter last year and the strongest first quarter since the pandemic peak. Average deal size hit $36.7M, the highest Rock Health has tracked in a single quarter since 2021. The kicker: Rock Health announced they're retiring their "AI deal" tracking category entirely, because AI is now table stakes in how digital health companies are built. That's a real milestone.

🤝 Acquisition - The WHOOP $575M Series G was the headline mega-deal of the quarter as the wearable giant eyes an IPO. Meanwhile OpenEvidence, which builds AI-powered search tools for clinicians, closed a $250M Series D. That's their third raise in quick succession, the kind of momentum that usually ends in a public listing.

🧬 TechBio - BioSpace's latest job market report flagged a rise in contract roles and a surge in M&A and IPO activity as early indicators of hiring growth ahead in biopharma. The FDA's push to reduce reliance on animal testing by endorsing AI-based computational models and in silico toxicity prediction is quietly one of the most significant regulatory shifts for TechBio this year. Less friction in preclinical development means faster pipelines.

❤️ Health equity - 59% of all Q1 capital went to just 12 companies. That concentration is a problem worth naming. When capital pools this tightly, early-stage founders building for underserved communities or niche populations get squeezed out before they can prove themselves. The HealthTechQuity lens here is: who isn't getting funded, and what does that mean for the populations they'd serve?

📈 Hiring signal - The biopharma job market is showing early recovery signs with contract roles ticking up and IPO/M&A activity creating downstream hiring. For life sciences talent professionals, the window between a funding announcement and first hires is getting shorter. If you're not in those conversations within the first two weeks of a raise, someone else is.

My take: Rock Health retiring the "AI deal" label isn't just a reporting change, it's a declaration. AI is no longer a differentiator in your pitch deck, it's the floor. The companies that stand out from here will be the ones who can show what their AI actually does in a real clinical or operational setting, not just that they have it.

Let's talk about #jobhunt for a sec... 
Attached is my LinkedIn Job tracker feature... 

First question I have:
Did you know this existed?

I only found out earlier this week and it is almost great. It doesn't have bulk actions, inner search, or count the amount of archived jobs (I have hundreds). 
The Interview status is just one. If you move to the interview. It doesn't allow you to change to "Rh call, hiring manager step, assignment step, offer" or whatever. So if you move one application to the interview, that's it

I see a lot of people saying that they track all applications, all phases of a hiring process, and I tend to take that as a grain of salt here. 

If you are doing this, would you say that you're being diligent and organized or that you maybe losing time to apply to more opportunities? Because there's another saying that if you see a job post that's more than 48h you're done.

I'm applying and interviewing since November 2025 since I knew my contract wouldn't be extended after January 2026.

I've updated my portfolio more than in 20+ years of my career with various feedback (thanks for each one of you that gave me one).

I've created at least 15 DIFFERENT slide presentations of the case studies that I have one my portfolio (and always asked WHY before committing to the slides).

I've done the crazy resume tailoring for each application. And I also applied using the same one for all to avoid losing time, even doing the tailor using AI (Claude is a lot better than ChatGPT btw).

I'm still applying, and not only on Linkedin, and I'm still interviewing. 

It is a marathon, not a 100m race... we'll get there... eventually.

What about you?

SaaS (software as a service) won because building was too expensive.

That's no longer true.

For years the equation was simple. Hiring developers cost too much. Building and maintaining software took too long. So businesses rented instead. Dynamics 365. Salesforce. ServiceNow. HubSpot. A subscription for every problem. SaaS won by default as much as by design.

AI accelerated development has changed that.

What used to take a team of developers months can now be built in weeks. Vibe coding and agentic development are putting real software creation within reach of organisations that would never have considered it before. And we're already seeing the consequences.

The first wave is micro-SaaS. Highly niche, purpose-built applications for specific use cases - created at a fraction of the cost of the sprawling platforms they're replacing. Smaller. Faster. Actually fit for purpose.

But the bigger shift is still coming.

Look at how businesses starting today approach technology. They aren't defaulting to SaaS stacks the way established businesses did. They're building leaner and more tailored from day one. Their software reflects how they actually operate - not how a platform forces them to.

Established businesses will take longer to shift. The contracts, the integrations, the habits built around existing tools. That transformation won't happen overnight.

But the direction is clear.

And I think it goes further than software.

The businesses that move fastest won't just be buying different tools. They'll be structured differently. Smaller, leaner teams - builders and problem solvers embedded directly into the business - designing solutions, building agents, owning the outcomes.

Technology stops being a department you manage. It becomes a capability you build in.

That's not just a shift in how businesses buy software.

It's a shift in how they're built.

Maersk just opened a fully automated distribution centre in Singapore. Autonomous robots handle the cargo. No human hands involved.
It's a glimpse of where logistics is heading across Asia-Pacific. But three legal basics are lagging behind.

1. Possessory liens get awkward fast

A warehouse operator's lien depends on possession — physically holding the goods until you're paid. When an autonomous system is doing all the handling, and the operator never touches the cargo, the question of whether "possession" is still meaningful hasn't been properly tested. Your warehouse contract needs to address this explicitly, because the common law assumption won't save you.

2. Liability fragments across the stack

When an autonomous robot damages or loses cargo, who's responsible? The operator, the robotics manufacturer, the software vendor, the systems integrator? In Mobley v Workday (N.D. Cal. 2024), a US federal court ruled that an AI vendor could be held directly liable as an "agent" — even though the vendor didn't make the final decision. The principle matters beyond hiring: if your autonomous system makes the call, the vendor behind it may carry liability too. That's a shift.

3. AI governance is a infrastructure question, not a policy document

Every autonomous decision generates data and runs compute somewhere. The EU's revised Product Liability Directive (applicable from December 2026) now treats software — including AI — as a "product," and says a system can become defective after sale if its machine learning behaviour changes. Governance means knowing where your models run, what they ingest, and whether you can audit the decision trail. A PDF policy on a shared drive doesn't get you there.

None of this is hypothetical. These systems are already operating.

A "Guide" for Anyone Who Wants to Deceive Their Employer.

Read this carefully — especially if you're the one responsible for making sure it doesn't happen.

Most fraud doesn't announce itself.
It doesn't arrive with red flags or warning signs. It walks through your front door as a new hire. It sits at a desk, attends your meetings, and quietly takes more than it gives.
Here are five ways people deceive employers — some during hiring, some from the inside. Most leave no digital footprint. Most require a trained eye to detect.

1. Reframing routine work as strategic leadership on a resume — a candidate who spent two years updating spreadsheets describes themselves as having "led data-driven financial transformation initiatives across the organization." Same experience. Different vocabulary. Entirely different hire.

2. Providing a personal contact as a professional reference — listed under a fabricated name, title, and company. The call goes smoothly. The feedback is glowing. The candidate moves forward. Nobody checks whether the number belongs to who it claims to.

3. Using AI in real time during video interviews to answer technical questions convincingly — with no preparation, no expertise, and no way for a standard interviewer to know.

4. Arranging undisclosed side payments with suppliers or vendors. A procurement manager steers contracts toward a specific vendor — not because of price or quality, but because of a private agreement made over a dinner that never appeared in any email. The vendor wins. The manager earns. The organization pays more than it should, and never knows why.

5. Falsifying expense reports in amounts small enough not to trigger immediate review — modest individually, significant over time, and remarkably difficult to catch without the right framework.

These aren't sophisticated schemes. They're quiet, practical, and far more common than most organizations realize.
The real problem isn't that people attempt them. It's that most organizations still rely on surface-level checks and basic trust.

A polished resume passes. A friendly reference confirms. Expense reports look clean.
And by the time the damage is visible — in lost margins, exposed data, or broken culture — the cost has already been paid.

The question isn't whether these risks exist inside your organization.
At any scale — from a growing mid-size business to a large enterprise — a single undetected hire or an undisclosed internal arrangement can quietly reshape your financial position, your reputation, and your culture in ways that take years to reverse.

The question is whether you have the intelligence layer to surface them before they compound.

In 2026, trust without verification isn't optimism. It's exposure.

P.R.D.S - Business Intelligence goes beyond the checkbox. Deep OSINT, behavioral profiling, pattern analysis, and forensic-level review — applied before a decision is made, or when something already doesn't feel right.

If you scan Anthropic’s careers page, you don’t see a lab quietly replacing humans; you see a full-stack enterprise software company racing to industrialise a new general-purpose capability, with dense hiring not just in research and infrastructure but in sales, finance, policy, safeguards and customer‑facing engineering. That apparent paradox - hiring salespeople, ops leaders and engineers while building tools that automate chunks of their work - is exactly what you’d expect when a technology shifts the production possibility frontier rather than simply cutting costs. 

The value isn’t “no salesperson” or “no programmer”; it is a smaller cohort of very high‑leverage people, each operating with an AI exoskeleton that lets them prosecute far more surface area than was humanly possible: engineers shipping and maintaining systems of previously unmanageable complexity, sales teams running deeply bespoke outreach and implementation plays at massive scale, support and success functions managing far larger books of business without losing fidelity. 

To make that work, you need humans who can orchestrate the tools, design workflows, enforce governance, negotiate contracts, manage risk, and translate messy organisational and regulatory constraints into something a model can actually optimise against. 

You also need category‑defining go‑to‑market and partnerships talent to wire these systems into existing enterprises, plus safeguards, security and policy teams to keep the whole thing within acceptable risk bounds. 

So the hiring pattern is a tell: we are not automating people out of the loop; we are rebuilding the loop so that the limiting factor becomes judgment, strategy and relationship‑building, while the models handle an ever‑growing share of the mechanical throughput.

The layoff wave in Web3 is brutal but the hiring patterns tell a different story

If you only read the headlines you'd think Web3 is shutting down entirely. 

But if you look at what's actually being hired for right now, a very different picture emerges. The industry isn't dying, it's restructuring & this tells you exactly where value is heading.

==> What's being cut

→ The 5-person marketing department running the same KOL & airdrop playbook from 2021 is being replaced by 1-2 people with AI tools & a strategy that ties to revenue.

→ 8 community managers across Discord, Telegram and Twitter is becoming 2-3 people with automation handling the routine work.

→ The "partnerships" role that produced logos on a website but no measurable business impact is gone. Every BD role now requires revenue attribution.

→ Multiple teams building overlapping features with no coordination. Bear markets force the question: do we actually need 30 engineers or do 12 focused ones ship faster?

→ Co-founders with vague operational roles, Chief Evangelist titles, advisory positions with compensation but no deliverables. The org chart is getting honest.

==> What's being quietly hired for

→ Every jurisdiction is building crypto frameworks simultaneously (MiCA, GENIUS Act, VASP Acts across Asia and Africa). Projects need people who understand this & most don't have them.

→ AI-integrated operations roles. Not "AI engineers" but operators who use AI to do the work of multiple people. 

→ Revenue-focused roles: Head of Revenue, Growth Lead with P&L responsibility, & Partnerships Manager with quota. Every title now has a number attached to it.

→ The people building the systems everything else runs on are not getting but raises. Security auditors and smart contract specialists are commanding premium compensation because one exploit destroys years of work.

→ As projects focus on specific geographies instead of "global" strategies, people who actually understand SSA, SEA, MENA or LATAM markets are in demand.

==> If you've been laid off here's the playbook

→ Don't panic-apply to 50 generic roles, reposition first. Ask yourself: what did I actually deliver that I can prove with data? Lead with outcomes.

→ Spend two weeks learning to integrate AI into whatever you do. 

→ If your old role doesn't exist anymore, don't look for the same title, look for the evolved version of what you did.

→ Consider emerging markets: Projects expanding into Africa, Southeast Asia & LATAM need people who understand those regions. If you have that knowledge, it's your competitive edge right now.

→ Build in public while you search. The person who's visibly adding value while between roles gets hired faster than the one sending silent applications.

Web3 is shedding roles built for a market that no longer exists and creating roles built for the market that's forming. Ensure you're positioned for the second category.

If you've been affected and want to talk about repositioning, reach out.

LLM development isn't "AI writes code"; it's a model shift: you're no longer a programmer, you manage the system through requirements, constraints, and tests. Code is now a side effect. The basic cycle is: Epic → contract → generation → tests → deploy. The key is to not give LLM freedom: prompt = contract (GOAL / CONSTRAINTS / DONE_WHEN), rules = hardcoded architecture, tests = sole control. Planning is done through domains and aggregates, not files—otherwise, you'll ruin the system. You don't read PRs anymore—you check behavior. Errors will increase—that means you need fallbacks, feature flags, and stranglers to prevent them from affecting the user. Bottleneck is shifting: we used to write code, now we manage requirements and tests. In short: manage the input, not the output, otherwise you'll be fixing the endless garbage you generated yourself.

I have bad news and a cold shower for you.

If you're still coding like it's 2019, I have bad news for you. While you're arguing about tabs vs. spaces, guys are already writing services by talking to AI. Claude Code isn't a "helper"; it's an intern-junior-middle-semi-team all at once. It works fast, sometimes it's slow, sometimes it's surprising. But most importantly, it speeds up development so much that the traditional "team → sprint → release" model starts to look like dial-up internet.

How to get started:
- Go to the Anthropic website (https://lnkd.in/gDgEETzG)
- Download Claude Code
- Open it and log in
- Message it:
Make a hello world with a front-end and back-end, so I can understand how you work and appreciate your power
- Wait 5 minutes
- Look at what it generated, and understand: this is just the beginning

Now, a cold shower

There will be more errors. If in classic development the noise level of production errors is ~2%, then here it's easily 5-6% plus the risk of incidents. Why? Because the generation speed is growing faster than our monitoring tools. This is a normal price for speedup. You just need to understand the limitations, maintain monitoring, alerts, and not have illusions that AI writes perfect code.

Another unexpected effect. When one person can generate as much code as an entire team, the classic "10 branches → 10 PRs → 10 conflicts → endless merges" model becomes a pain. I don't know what to do about it yet, and it seems few people know...

And the speedup—no joke. A very rough estimate: 10 x 7 ≈ 70 times. What a team of 7 used to do in a 2-week (10-day) sprint can now be done almost single-handedly. Not because you've become a genius, but because there's an endlessly patient code generator sitting next to you.

News for those who wrote code "by feel." If you don't understand units and integration tests, don't know how to divide a system into domains, and don't understand context boundaries—you'll find yourself in a long and painful hell. Because AI writes code perfectly, but you have to maintain the architecture. And yes, architectural tests, contract tests, automated domain boundary checks, and a bunch of other fun stuff are already appearing.

So the new developer skill sounds simple: become an architect. Everyone wanted to be one, right? Here's your chance. Because a developer who manages AI through architecture will be worth as much as a team. And someone who simply writes functions will be arguing with autocomplete while the market races ahead.
